coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional cellulose nanofibrils-based composite film with superior mechanical strength and flame retardancy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Xinmiao
Qiao, Jianzheng
Liu, Mingyi
Zhang, Yuting
Ma, Qiang
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Flammability remains a major challenge for the widespread application of cellulose-based films. To overcome this limitation, we developed a high-performance composite film (CCTCo) with superior mechanical strength and flame retardancy. The film was based on cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), titanate nanofibers, carbon dots, and Co-based metal-organic frameworks (Co-MOFs), and was fabricated using a novel strategy, SPFN. The CCTCo achieved a tensile strength of 39.8 MPa and an elongation at break of 37.5 %, outperforming most cellulose-based nanocomposites. Furthermore, it demonstrated exceptional flame retardancy, with an 86.2 % reduction in peak heat release rate and a 44.1 % decrease in total heat release compared to the CNs film. Mechanistic studies confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, SPFN, which combines surface charge inversion, physical morphology modulation, functional nanoparticle incorporation, and network structure construction. This study presents a novel strategy to overcoming the flammability limitations of CNs-based films, unlocking their potential for next-generation multifunctional films, particularly for sustainable packaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanofibrils
Film
Metal-organic frameworks

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFC3010103-2), Hunan Province Graduate Research Innovation Project (Grant No. CX20240726), and 2024 Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Fund Project of Central South University of Forestry and Technology (Grant No. 2024CX02023). The authors would like to thank Jialing Wu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.
This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFD2201405 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-d) SEM structures of CNs, CDs@CNs, TNs, and CCTCo-2. (e) FTIR analysis. (f) XRD curves. (g) XPS survey spectrum. (h, i) Ti2p, and Co2p XPS spectra. (j) Interactions among the components in CCTCo-2.

(a) Stress-strain curves. (b, c) Tensile strength and elongation at break. (d) Comparison between the CNs film and CCTCo-2. (e) Comparison of tensile strengths of the CCTCo-2 and previously reported cellulose-based composite materials. (f) Various complex shapes. (g, h) SEM images of the CNs film and the CCTCo-2. (i) The proposed fracture mechanism of CCTCo-2.

(a) Testing conditions. (b) Sample temperature versus heating time curves. (c) Temperature differences. (d) Infrared thermal images. (e) Temperature-time curves. (f) The proposed thermal insulation mechanism.
(a) TG curves. (b) DTG curves. (c) Char residues.
Digital photos of the prepared samples during UL-94 tests.

(a) HRR, (b) THR, (c) SPR, (d) TSP, (e) COP, (f) CO2P versus time profiles. (g) FPI values. (h) FIGRA values. (i) Radar charts of the CNs film and CCTCo-2. (j) LOI values. (k) Comparison of combustion properties.
(a) Digital photos and SEM micrographs of residual char. (b) FTIR spectra of residual char. (c-e) C1s XPS spectra of residual char. (f-h) Raman spectra of residual char.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional cellulose nanofibrils-based composite film with superior mechanical strength and flame retardancy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flammability remains a major challenge for the widespread application of cellulose-based films. To overcome this limitation, we developed a high-performance composite film (CCTCo) with superior mechanical strength and flame retardancy. The film was based on cellulose nanofibrils (CNs), titanate nanofibers, carbon dots, and Co-based metal-organic frameworks (Co-MOFs), and was fabricated using a novel strategy, SPFN. The CCTCo achieved a tensile strength of 39.8 MPa and an elongation at break of 37.5 %, outperforming most cellulose-based nanocomposites. Furthermore, it demonstrated exceptional flame retardancy, with an 86.2 % reduction in peak heat release rate and a 44.1 % decrease in total heat release compared to the CNs film. Mechanistic studies confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, SPFN, which combines surface charge inversion, physical morphology modulation, functional nanoparticle incorporation, and network structure construction. This study presents a novel strategy to overcoming the flammability limitations of CNs-based films, unlocking their potential for next-generation multifunctional films, particularly for sustainable packaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the increasing demand for sustainable materials has propelled cellulose and its derivatives to the forefront of research in packaging applications. Among these, cellulose nanofibrils (CNs) have emerged as a promising candidate due to their renewability, biodegradability, and exceptional mechanical properties. CNs exhibit high tensile strength and an ultralight structure, offering mechanical performance comparable to conventional materials while significantly reducing packaging weight, thus lowering transportation costs [1]. However, a critical challenge limits the widespread use of CNs-based films: their intrinsic flammability. This vulnerability presents significant safety risks, particularly under ignition-prone conditions encountered during transportation, storage, and use [2]. The combustion of CNs-based films not only undermines their structural integrity but also exacerbates fire hazards, highlighting the urgent need for innovative flame-retardant solutions. Overcoming this limitation is crucial for realizing the full potential of CNs-based films in sustainable packaging applications.

To address the inherent flammability of CNs-based films, research has primarily focused on incorporating various inorganic fillers, such as montmorillonite [3], aluminum oxide [4], titanium dioxide [5], magnesium hydroxide [6], and aluminum and calcium phosphates [7,8]. These fillers serve as critical agents for enhancing flame retardancy by forming protective thermal barriers and promoting char formation during combustion. For example, Fang et al. engineered antimony tin oxide/CNs/polyvinyl alcohol thermal insulation films, which exhibited improved flame retardancy [9]. Likewise, Xia et al. [10] demonstrated that incorporating hydroxyapatite nanowires into CNs films effectively suppressed flammability. Despite these advancements, a persistent challenge remains: weak interfacial interactions between inorganic fillers and CNs often lead to filler aggregation, undermining the uniformity and mechanical properties of the resulting films. This trade-off between mechanical strength and flame retardancy underscores a fundamental limitation of current approaches. Thus, developing CNs-based films that simultaneously offer high mechanical performance and enhanced flame retardancy requires innovative strategies that extend beyond conventional filler incorporation.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based nanomaterials, exhibit excellent chemical stability and biocompatibility, making them versatile candidates for advanced material applications [11]. Their inherent thermal stability further positions them as promising candidates for flame retardant applications [12]. Liu et al. [13] demonstrated the potential of CDs as eco-friendly flame retardants in polymeric foams and fibers, thus paving the way for their integration into CNs-based films. Similarly, titanate nanomaterials have emerged as exceptional flame retardants, offering superior thermal stability by resisting decomposition and deformation under high-temperature conditions [14]. Moreover, they facilitate the formation of effective thermal insulation layers that minimize heat transfer. These synergistic effects make titanate nanomaterials suitable for the development of flame-retardant CNs-based films.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a rapidly evolving class of porous crystalline materials, have attracted significant attention for their flame-retardant properties, which arise from their exceptional porosity and thermal stability [15]. The intricate pore network within MOFs plays a crucial role in fire suppression by absorbing and isolating heat and oxygen, effectively slowing the spread of flames and reducing heat release. Additionally, MOFs exhibit catalytic activity that promotes the formation of protective char layers, which act as thermal barriers to further inhibit flame spread [16]. Among various types of MOFs, cobalt-based frameworks (Co-MOFs) are recognized as one of the most effective flame retardants [17]. Xu et al. [18] demonstrated that incorporating as little as 3 wt% Co-MOFs into a composite material resulted in a reduction of the heat release rate by over 40 %, highlighting their exceptional flame-retardant performance. These unique properties position Co-MOFs as promising candidates for enhancing the flame retardancy of CNs-based films.

This study addresses the challenge of achieving both high mechanical performance and enhanced flame retardancy in CNs-based films through the introduction of a novel strategy, SPFN. A high-performance composite film, referred to as CCTCo, was developed by using CNs, titanate nanofibers (TNs), CDs, and Co-MOFs. The SPFN strategy combines several critical approaches, including surface charge inversion, physical morphology modulation, functional nanoparticle incorporation, and network structure construction. The CCTCo was systematically characterized in terms of its morphology, structure, and mechanical properties, demonstrating outstanding mechanical strength and flame retardancy. Mechanistic investigations provided further insights into the role of SPFN, offering a comprehensive framework for the design of advanced cellulose-based films with optimized performance.

The bleached wood pulp (spruce) used in this study was sourced from Dalian Yangrun Trading Co., Ltd. (China). This pulp predominantly consists of cellulose, with trace amounts of residual lignin. Additional details regarding the chemical reagents used in this study are provided in the Supplementary Materials.
The synthesis of CNs was carried out according to the procedure outlined in the Supplementary Materials.

A precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 5.04 g of citric acid and 6.43 mL of ethylenediamine in 120 mL of ultrapure water. The solution underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h in a sealed autoclave, yielding a dispersion of CDs [19]. The dispersion was purified through centrifugation at 4000 rpm and dialyzed in ultrapure water for 24 h.

To prepare the CDs@CNs, 30 g of CNs suspension (1.0 wt%) was mixed with 15 mL of acetate buffer (pH 5). The mixture was thoroughly stirred, followed by the addition of 0.09 g of N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC). Subsequently, 0.34 g of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was introduced, and stirring was continued for 30 min [20]. The resulting mixture was then combined with 0.8 g of CDs dispersion (0.5 wt%), and stirring continued for 12 h. The final CDs@CNs suspension was purified by dialysis in ultrapure water for 36 h.

TNs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using titanium oxysulfate (TiOSO4) as the precursor [21]. Specifically, 1.43 g of TiOSO4 was dissolved in 10.66 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 26.66 mL of 15 M NaOH solution was then added. The mixture was sonicated to ensure complete mixing and then aged at 200 °C for 48 h. After aging, the product was washed with water until the supernatant reached a neutral pH of 7. The resulting white precipitate was collected and dried overnight at 60 °C to obtain the TNs.

Co-MOFs were synthesized as follows. First, 3 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 30 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF), and 0.75 mmol of terephthalic acid (TPA) was added to the solution. The mixture was stirred vigorously until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The solution was then sealed in an autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 14 h. The resulting purple precipitate was washed several times with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water. After drying for 12 h, the Co-MOFs were obtained [22].

The CCTCo was prepared by adding 0.047 g of TNs to 3 g of a CDs@CNs suspension. The mixture was stirred for 2 h to ensure uniform dispersion. Then, 0.005 g of Co-MOFs was introduced, and the mixture was sonicated for 1 h. Following this, 0.5 mL of epichlorohydrin (ECH) was added as a crosslinking agent, and the solution was rapidly stirred to ensure thorough mixing. The resulting composite was cast into a Petri dish and vacuum-dried at 55 °C for 10 h, forming CCTCo with a thickness of approximately 1 mm. To investigate the effect of Co-MOFs loading, composite films with varying Co-MOFs amounts (0.001 g, 0.005 g, 0.010 g, and 0.015 g) were prepared following the same procedure and labeled as CCTCo-x (where x = 1, 2, 3, and 4). Control films were also prepared for comparison: one without Co-MOFs (labeled CCT) and one without CDs, TNs, and Co-MOFs (labeled CNs film). The formulations of the different samples are summarized in Table S1.
Detailed information on the instruments and their respective parameters used in this study is provided in the Supplementary Materials.

The synthesis pathway for the CCTCo is outlined in Fig. 1 . The process began with the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. Meanwhile, CNs were prepared via TEMPO-mediated oxidation, followed by mechanical homogenization, resulting in well-dispersed nanofibers. Thermally stable TNs were then synthesized using a hydrothermal method, yielding high-aspect-ratio fibrous structures. Following this, CDs were covalently grafted onto the CNs to form CDs@CNs, ensuring stable integration of the two components. These CDs@CNs were subsequently combined with TNs to create a composite with strong interfacial compatibility. To further enhance the material's flame-retardant properties, Co-MOFs were incorporated into the interstitial spaces between CDs@CNs and TNs. Finally, to further stabilize the structure, a three-dimensional (3D) network was formed by crosslinking the CDs@CNs with ECH, resulting in the formation of the CCTCo.

The morphological evolution during the synthesis of CCTCo-2 and its precursors was systematically examined. The CNs exhibited a uniform fibrous morphology with smooth surfaces (Fig. 2a). Upon crosslinking with CDs, the resulting CDs@CNs displayed rough and irregular fiber surfaces (Fig. 2b). The TNs maintained an elongated fibrous morphology with smooth surfaces (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, the Co-MOFs exhibited a layered crystalline structure, as shown in Fig. S1. The final CCTCo-2 displayed a well-defined 3D network structure (Fig. 2d).

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the functional groups of CCTCo-2 and its precursors (Fig. 2e). The spectra of CNs and CDs@CNs displayed characteristic peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 1053 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of –OH and CO groups, respectively [23]. Notably, a new peak appeared at 1650 cm−1 in the CDs@CNs spectrum, attributed to the –CO–NH– group [24]. The FTIR spectrum of TNs exhibited a distinct peak at 910 cm−1, indicative of the TiO bond stretching vibration, which was a hallmark of the fibrous titanate structure [25]. The Co-MOFs spectrum displayed multiple peaks between 600 and 1500 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of the imidazole ring. Additional peaks at 2929 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 were assigned to the aromatic CH stretching and the CN stretching of 2-methylimidazole, respectively [26]. After the synthesis of CCTCo-2, several spectral changes were observed. The disappearance of the CO peak at 1053 cm−1 indicated successful crosslinking between CDs@CNs and ECH. Moreover, the attenuation of the TiO peak at 910 cm−1 suggested strong interactions between TNs and CDs@CNs [25]. The reduced intensity of the characteristic Co-MOFs peaks further corroborated the successful incorporation of Co-MOFs into the CCTCo-2.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CCTCo-2 and its precursors are shown in Fig. 2 f. Both CNs and CDs@CNs exhibited characteristic peaks corresponding to cellulose [27]. Notably, the absence of additional peaks in the CDs@CNs pattern confirmed that the crosslinking of CDs did not disrupt the crystalline structure of cellulose. TNs displayed sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 10.4°, 24.9°, 29.6°, 34.4°, 35.9°, 38.8°, and 48.4°, corresponding to the (100), (201), (003), (002), (301), (013), and (020) planes [28]. Similarly, Co-MOFs exhibited distinct peaks at 2θ = 8.4°, 10.3°, 16.8°, and 28.3°, corresponding to the (022), (110), (310), and (004) planes [22]. In contrast, the XRD pattern of CCTCo-2 revealed notable changes. The cellulose-related peaks from CDs@CNs were attenuated, likely due to crosslinking with ECH. Additionally, the reduced intensity of the Co-MOFs peaks further indicated their successful integration into the CCTCo-2.

To explore the elemental composition and chemical states of CCTCo-2, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed (Fig. 2 g). The survey spectrum confirmed the presence of five elements: carbon (C), nitrogen (N), titanium (Ti), oxygen (O), and cobalt (Co), consistent with the expected composition of the composite. A more detailed examination of the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 2 h) revealed two distinct peaks at binding energies of 458.2 eV and 463.9 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively [29], which are characteristic of the Ti4+ oxidation state. Similarly, the high-resolution Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 2i) showed four deconvoluted peaks: two primary peaks at 781.1 eV (Co 2p3/2) and 796.1 eV (Co 2p1/2), along with two satellite peaks at 786.5 eV and 802.5 eV [30].

The interactions among the components in CCTCo-2 are schematically depicted in Fig. 2j. Covalent bonding between the –COOH groups of CNs and the –NH2 groups of CDs facilitated the formation of the CDs@CNs. The TNs, which primarily contained Ti3O7 2− ions, interacted with the positively charged CDs@CNs through electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding. Furthermore, Co-MOFs integrated into the 3D network of the composite via hydrogen bonding, which enhanced the overall structural integrity of CCTCo-2.

The mechanical properties of the prepared samples were systematically evaluated. The stress-strain curve for the CNs film demonstrated a tensile strength of 6.89 MPa and an elongation at break of 24.4 % (Fig. 3a). The incorporation of TNs into the CNs matrix led to a significant enhancement in mechanical performance, with the tensile strength increasing to 13.4 MPa and the elongation at break reaching 26.4 % in the CCT. These improvements are attributed to the enhanced electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and increased entanglement between TNs and CDs@CNs, which strengthened the overall matrix. Further enhancement was achieved by adding Co-MOFs to the CCT matrix. The optimal mechanical performance was observed in CCTCo-2, with 12 % Co-MOFs, which exhibited a remarkable tensile strength of 39.8 MPa and an elongation at break of 37.5 % (Fig. 3b-c). This corresponded to a 478 % increase in tensile strength and a 53.7 % improvement in elongation at break compared to the CNs film (Fig. 3d). However, excessive loading of Co-MOFs resulted in a slight reduction in mechanical performance, likely due to particle agglomeration and accumulation within the CCTCo matrix, which hindered the material's integrity.

CCTCo-2 outperformed other cellulose-based composite materials in terms of tensile strength (Fig. 3e, Table S2). Its flexibility and processability were further demonstrated by shaping it into intricate forms using origami and paper-cutting techniques (Fig. 3f). SEM images revealed a uniform distribution of TNs in the CNs matrix and a denser, more interconnected network in CCTCo-2, with Co-MOFs encapsulated within the 3D structure (Fig. 3g-h). This denser network was crucial for enhancing the mechanical strength of CCTCo-2.

A fracture mechanism under tensile stress was proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 3i. Upon the application of tensile force, the dense network structure deformed and eventually fractured. The process involved slippage between CNs chains, weakening hydrogen bonds, and disrupting the interactions between CDs@CNs and TNs. These disruptions led to the fracture of the organic-inorganic interpenetrating network, thus highlighting the mechanical response of CCTCo to applied stress.

Thermal insulation is a critical property for evaluating composite films. To assess this characteristic, a sample (10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm) was placed on a heating plate set to 80 °C, and its surface temperature distribution was monitored using infrared thermal imager (Fig. 4a). After 20 min of heating, the top surface temperatures of the CNs film, CCT, and CCTCo-2 were 57.2 °C, 55.5 °C, and 49.6 °C, respectively (Fig. 4b). The significantly lower temperature of CCTCo-2 demonstrated its superior thermal insulation, attributed to the incorporation of TNs and Co-MOFs. The temperature differences (ΔT) between the film surface and the heating plate, shown in Fig. 4c, further supported this trend. CCTCo-2 exhibited the highest ΔT of 30.4 °C, outperforming the CNs film (22.8 °C) and CCT (24.5 °C). These results were further confirmed by infrared thermal imaging (Fig. 4d), which emphasized the superior thermal insulation of CCTCo-2. Additionally, the dynamic thermal regulation of CCTCo-2 was assessed through cyclic heating (25–80 °C) and cooling (80–25 °C) tests. The resulting temperature-time curves (Fig. 4e) confirmed that CCTCo-2 maintained excellent thermal insulation under cyclic thermal conditions.

The proposed thermal insulation mechanism for CCTCo-2 is illustrated in Fig. 4f. The incorporation of Co-MOFs and the formation of an organic-inorganic interpenetrating network resulted in a highly porous and interconnected structure. This architecture effectively reduced thermal radiation, enhanced heat reflection, and hindered solid-state heat conduction [31], thereby imparting CCTCo-2 with exceptional thermal insulation.

The thermal stability of the CNs film, CCT, and CCTCo-2 was evaluated using thermogravimetric (TG) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) analysis under a nitrogen atmosphere (Fig. 5a-b). The key thermal properties are summarized in Table S3.

The CNs film exhibited a 5 % weight loss temperature (T5%) of 77.2 °C, attributed to water evaporation [32], followed by a peak mass loss rate temperature (Tmax) of 282.6 °C during cellulose decomposition [33]. At 800 °C, the CNs film left a residual char of 12.1 wt%. In contrast, the T5% values for the modified samples were significantly higher: 95.3 °C for CCCo, 96.1 °C for CCT, and 120.3 °C for CCTCo-2, indicating a marked improvement in thermal stability. While the Tmax values of CCCo (275.7 °C), CCT (269.4 °C) and CCTCo-2 (264.4 °C) were slightly lower than that of the CNs film, this reduction was attributed to the enhanced char formation promoted by the presence of TNs and Co-MOFs [34]. Incorporation of TNs and Co-MOFs also reduced the maximum mass loss rate (Rmax) from −1.01 wt%/°C in the CNs film to −0.35 wt%/°C, −0.27 wt%/°C and − 0.23 wt%/°C in CCCo, CCT and CCTCo-2, respectively. Additionally, the temperature at which the maximum rate of mass loss (TRmax) occurred was delayed for the modified samples, with values of 309.4 °C, 311.2 °C and 322.0 °C for CCCo, CCT and CCTCo-2, respectively, reflecting a delay in thermal degradation [35]. The char residue at 800 °C showed a significant increase in the modified samples, reaching 35.8 wt% for CCCo, 42.2 wt% for CCT and 48.9 wt% for CCTCo-2, further corroborating the improved thermal stability of these materials (Fig. 5c).

The flame-retardant performance of the prepared samples was evaluated by exposing them to a butane flame. As shown in Fig. S2 and Movie S1a-d, the CNs film exhibited poor flame resistance, suffering immediate damage upon exposure and burning almost completely within 5 s. In contrast, CCCo, CCT, and CCTCo-2 demonstrated significantly enhanced flame resistance. Notably, CCTCo-2 maintained its structural integrity and contour stability even after 50 s of exposure, highlighting its superior flame-retardant properties.

The flame-retardant performance of the prepared samples was evaluated by exposing them to a butane flame. As shown in Fig. S2 and Movie S1a-d, the CNs film exhibited poor flame resistance, suffering immediate damage upon exposure and burning almost completely within 5 s. In contrast, CCCo, CCT, and CCTCo-2 demonstrated significantly enhanced flame resistance. Notably, CCTCo-2 maintained its structural integrity and contour stability even after 50 s of exposure, highlighting its superior flame-retardant properties.

Further evaluation was performed through UL-94 tests (Fig. 6 ), with detailed results summarized in Table S4. The CNs film showed high flammability, with the flame spreading rapidly and continuing to burn after initial ignition. During combustion, significant molten drips were produced, preventing the CNs film from achieving a UL-94 rating. On the other hand, both CCCo and CCT self-extinguished after the first ignition, with burn durations of 6.8 and 3.0 s, respectively. After the second ignition, both samples self-extinguished within 2.4 and 1.8 s, achieving a V-0 rating. CCTCo-2 exhibited the best flame-retardant performance, with the flame self-extinguishing almost immediately after both ignitions (within 1.0 and 0.5 s, respectively), without any molten drips. This exceptional performance enabled CCTCo-2 to achieve the highest V-0 rating, further emphasizing its superior flame-retardant properties.

Cone calorimetry tests (CCT) were conducted to evaluate the flame-retardant properties of the prepared samples under simulated real-world conditions. The heat release rate (HRR) and total heat release (THR) values for all samples are shown in Fig. 7a-b and Table S5. The CNs film exhibited a peak HRR (pHRR) of 55.74 kW/m2 and a THR of 1.27 MJ/m2. In contrast, the incorporation of TNs and Co-MOFs led to significant reductions in both parameters: the pHRR decreased to 38.72 kW/m2 for CCCo, 7.78 kW/m2 for CCT and 7.67 kW/m2 for CCTCo-2, representing reductions of 30.5 %, 86.0 % and 86.2 %, respectively. Similarly, the THR values decreased by 27.6 %, 37.8 % and 44.1 % for CCCo, CCT, and CCTCo-2, respectively, relative to the CNs film.

In addition to heat release, smoke emission is a critical challenge in fire safety. The smoke production rate (SPR) and total smoke production (TSP) for the prepared samples are shown in Fig. 7c-d and Table S5. The CNs film released a significant amount of smoke, with the peak SPR (pSPR) and TSP values of 0.060 m2/s and 0.48 m2, respectively. However, the incorporation of TNs and Co-MOFs resulted in substantial reductions in smoke emissions [36,37]. For CCTCo-2, the pSPR and TSP were reduced to 0.008 m2/s and 0.04 m2, corresponding to reductions of 86.7 % and 91.7 %, respectively, compared to the CNs film. This superior smoke suppression was attributed to the catalytic effects of TNs and Co-MOFs [38], which facilitated the formation of dense and stable char layers, effectively hindering smoke release [39].

The reduction in harmful gas emissions is another important aspect of fire safety. The carbon monoxide (COP) and carbon dioxide (CO2P) production for CCTCo-2 were reduced by 45.1 % and 96.9 %, respectively, compared to the CNs film (Fig. 7e-f). Fire propagation and growth rates were assessed using the fire propagation index (FPI, TTI/pHRR) and the fire growth rate index (FIGRA, pHRR/t-pHRR) [40]. The FPI for CCTCo-2 showed a remarkable 986 % increase compared to the CNs film, indicating a significant reduction in flammability (Fig. 7g) [41]. Additionally, the FIGRA for CCTCo-2 decreased by 98.6 %, from 5.574 to 0.078 kW/(m2·s), demonstrating a substantial reduction in the flame spread rate (Fig. 7h). Together, these indices confirm the enhanced fire safety of CCTCo-2 (Fig. 7i) [42]. Further flame resistance testing revealed a significant increase in the limiting oxygen index (LOI), with CCTCo-2 achieving an LOI of 57.8 %, compared to 25.2 % for the CNs film (Fig. 7j).

The flame-retardant performance of CCTCo-2 was further compared with other flame-retardant materials reported in the literature, as summarized in Table S6 and illustrated in Fig. 7k. CCTCo-2 outperformed most materials in terms of overall flame-retardant performance. However, PAZn3@DW demonstrated a greater reduction in both pHRR and SPR compared to CCTCo-2. In contrast, CCTCo-2 exhibited superior LOI performance compared to PAZn3@DW. Overall, CCTCo-2 demonstrated more balanced and superior flame-retardant properties.

The char morphology and structure of the prepared samples after CCT are shown in Fig. 8a. The CNs film formed a loose, porous char layer that allowed the diffusion of flammable gases, which contributed to its high flammability. In contrast, the incorporation of TNs and Co-MOFs resulted in the formation of a dense, intact char layer, devoid of surface pores.

FTIR spectra of the residual char from CNs film, CCCo, CCT, and CCTCo-2 after CCT are presented in Fig. 8b. The peak at 1635 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CC bond, while the absorption peak at 1250 cm−1 was attributed to the CC stretching vibration [43]. Notably, the intensity of the CC bond in CCTCo-2 was significantly higher compared to other samples, indicating improved stability of the residual char [44].

To further elucidate the structure of the char layer, the residual char after CCT was also analyzed using XPS. The high-resolution C1s spectra revealed significant changes in the relative intensities of the CC, CO, and CO components (Fig. 8c-f). The CC peak area ratio in CCTCo-2 increased to 61.74 %, showing a 6.28 % improvement compared to the CNs film (Table S7), indicating enhanced char stability [45]. Additionally, the COx/Ca ratio, which represents the ratio of oxidized to unoxidized carbon, decreased progressively with the incorporation of TN and Co-MOFs, suggesting improved resistance to thermal oxidation. The lowest COx/Ca ratio in CCTCo-2 indicated that TNs and Co-MOFs catalyzed the degradation process, promoting the formation of a dense and thermally stable char layer [46].

Raman spectra (Fig. 8g-j) were used to assess the graphitization degree of the residual char. The ID/IG ratio, indicative of graphitization, decreased from 2.15 in the CNs film to 2.03, 1.96 and 1.70 in CCCo, CCT and CCTCo-2, respectively, confirming an increase in graphitization [47]. The enhanced graphitization degree of the char layer provided better protection against flame erosion, further contributing to the superior flame-retardant properties of the CCTCo-2 [48].

To further investigate the structural information of volatile products, thermogravimetric analysis coupled with Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (TG-FTIR) was performed. The FTIR spectra of the pyrolysis products of CCTCo-2 at various temperatures are shown in Fig. S3. A broad peak at 3736 cm−1 was observed, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of the –OH group, likely originating from water vapor and other hydroxyl-containing compounds. Additionally, peaks at 2360 cm−1 and 2140 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO2 and CO, respectively [49]. A signal at 3568 cm−1 was also detected, indicating the stretching vibration of NH3 [50]. These results confirmed the release of various gases, including H2O, CO, CO2, and NH3.

The implementation of the SPFN strategy significantly improved both the mechanical properties and flame retardancy of CCTCo, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . This strategy integrates multiple mechanisms, including surface charge inversion, physical morphology modulation, functional nanoparticle incorporation, and network structure construction.

Surface charge inversion facilitated electrostatic interactions between the positively charged CDs@CNs and the negatively charged TNs, enhancing the structural integrity of CCTCo. Moreover, the modulation of physical morphology increased the entanglement of the components, while the incorporation of functional nanoparticles and network construction further enhanced the tensile strength of the composite. The SPFN-induced porous structure played a crucial role in reducing thermal radiation and enhancing heat reflection, serving as an effective barrier to solid-state heat conduction, thus providing excellent thermal insulation. The uniform distribution of TNs and Co-MOFs within the composite ensured a strong integration with CNs, promoting the formation of a dense and thermally stable char layer. This char layer effectively impeded heat and oxygen transfer, slowing material degradation and reducing heat release during combustion. Furthermore, the porous Co-MOFs trapped volatile decomposition products, which mitigated the release of flammable gases and slowed flame propagation.

In conclusion, the combined structural and catalytic effects of the SPFN strategy endowed CCTCo with superior mechanical strength and flame retardancy. These results highlight the potential of SPFN as a versatile and powerful strategy for the design of advanced CNs-based films.

The novel SPFN strategy, which integrates surface charge inversion, physical morphology modulation, functional nanoparticle incorporation, and network structure formation, has proven to be highly effective in enhancing the properties of CNs-based films. The resulting CCTCo demonstrated substantial improvements in both mechanical strength and flame retardancy, effectively addressing the inherent flammability limitations of cellulose-based films. CCTCo exhibited a tensile strength of 39.8 MPa and an elongation at break of 37.5 %, outperforming most previously reported cellulose-based composites. Moreover, its flame retardancy was markedly enhanced, as evidenced by substantial reductions in pHRR (86.2 %), THR (44.1 %), pSPR (86.7 %), and TSP (91.7 %) compared to the CNs film. These results highlight the significant potential of the SPFN strategy in advancing the development of cellulose-based films with superior mechanical and flame-retardant properties.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.

Combustion test of CNs film.
Video S1a
Video S1b
Combustion test of CCCo.
Video S1b
Video S1c
Combustion test of CCT.
Video S1c
Video S1d
Combustion test of CCTCo-2.
Video S1d
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.140917.

Xinmiao Qi: Investigation, Data curation. Jianzheng Qiao: Investigation, Data curation. Mingyi Liu: Validation, Software. Yuting Zhang: Software. Qiang Ma: Software. Xin Guo: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yiqiang Wu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
